title: A Pictorial Krater from Philistine Ashkelon, in Up to the Gates of Ekron: Essays on the Archaeology and History of the Eastern Mediterranean in Honor of Seymour Gitin (eds. S. W. Crawford et al.)
author: Lawrence E. Stager; Penelope A. Mountjoy
publisher: Jerusalem: Israel Exploration Society / W. F. Albright Institute of Archaeological Research
allowedUseNotes: Copyrighted festschrift chapter. Summaries in project's own words only; no image reproductions.
summary: Publication of a painted Philistine Bichrome krater excavated at Ashkelon (Grid 38, Phase 18) depicting a warrior wearing the feathered/plumed headdress otherwise known from the Egyptian Medinet Habu reliefs. Because the vessel is locally made Philistine pottery rather than Egyptian royal art, Stager reads it (following his own earlier 1998 discussion) as effectively a Philistine self-portrait of a warrior in this headgear. The krater belongs to the 'ripe'/mature Philistine Bichrome pottery phase, conventionally placed in the later 12th–11th century BCE — extending the attested date range of the headdress motif locally within Philistia beyond the single c. 1175 BCE Egyptian relief moment, though still within the coastal plain, not the highlands.
confidenceNotes: Full citation (authors, chapter title, festschrift title/editors/publisher/year) confirmed via multiple corroborating search results; could not obtain the exact internal page range for the book chapter itself (publisher/library listings found did not surface pagination), and the krater's precise excavation-phase date (the 'ripe Bichrome' phase is usually bracketed c. 1150–1050 BCE by excavators, but exact end-date is a live chronology question tied to the wider Iron I/IIA debate) was not independently page-checked against the original publication. TO VERIFY: exact page range and the excavators' own stated date bracket for Phase 18.
